methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $182M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 1 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Fuse Energy

data.narrative:
Standard Nuclear, a Series A nuclear energy company founded by Kurt Terrani, operates within a sector represented by 3 public comps and 8 private peers including Fuse Energy, Oklo, TerraPower, X-Energy, and Kairos Power. The company has raised $182M to date.

The valuation analysis employs stage-progression methodology, the only applicable triangulation technique given insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage nuclear peers meeting statistical thresholds. Using total capital raised as a proxy base ($182M) and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical for Series A advancement yields $364M. A peer-median approach was not viable; while 8 nuclear energy companies exist in the comp universe, filtering for same-stage or similar-tier counterparts produced fewer than three datapoints, making median-based extrapolation unreliable. Relying on all-sector medians would introduce unacceptable sector-specificity bias.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $309M (low) to $419M (high), with a median of $364M. This narrow range reflects reliance on a single methodology rather than convergence across multiple approaches. Key uncertainties include the appropriate step-up multiplier for nuclear energy specifically—the 2.0x assumption may not reflect sector-specific investor behavior—and the representativeness of total raised as a valuation proxy absent comparable exit or revenue data. Additional comparable transactions or financial performance metrics would materially improve precision.
